items.title: Fraud detection model comparison
items.scenario: A bank trains two scorers on the same validation set: Model A (logistic regression) and Model B (gradient-boosted trees). Both hit 94% accuracy at threshold 0.5, but ROC-AUC is 0.91 vs 0.87. The team selects Model A because it ranks known fraud cases higher when the ops team later lowers the threshold to catch more fraud.
items.explanation: Accuracy at one threshold can tie while ranking differs. AUC captures separability across all cutoffs — the right metric for picking a scorer before threshold tuning.

items.title: Medical screening (diabetes)
items.scenario: Clinic compares two risk models on 10,000 patients (8% diabetic). Model X: AUC 0.84. Model Y: AUC 0.79. Clinicians still pick a screening threshold separately (e.g. recall ≥ 90%), but AUC justifies preferring X during model selection.
items.explanation: ROC evaluates how well risk scores order sick patients above healthy ones. Threshold for referral is a downstream clinical choice, not baked into AUC.

items.title: Spam filter A/B test
items.scenario: Filter v2 outputs P(spam|email). On a labeled holdout, v2 AUC = 0.96 vs v1 AUC = 0.93. Product raises the spam threshold from 0.5 to 0.8 to cut false positives while v2's higher AUC shows better underlying ranking if they need to relax the cutoff later.
items.explanation: Logistic-regression probabilities feed ROC directly. AUC validates the scorer; the product team moves along the ROC curve by changing threshold.

items.title: SVM with decision scores
items.scenario: An SVM trained with RBF kernel exposes decision_function scores (not probabilities). sklearn.metrics.roc_auc_score(y_true, scores) returns 0.88 on validation — sufficient to compare kernels before Platt scaling for calibrated probabilities.
items.explanation: ROC needs a ranking score, not strictly probabilities. SVM margins work; probability calibration is optional for AUC.
